title: The Spiritual Teacher Who Survived Kundalini Crisis: What They Learned

body:
The Spiritual Teacher Who Survived Kundalini Crisis: What They Learned

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, staring at your ceiling, or maybe you're curled up somewhere trying to make sense of the chaos inside you. You're scared. You feel like you're losing your mind, or maybe you've already lost it. Everything you thought was real is crumbling, and you don't know who to talk to. I get it. I've been there. That's why I'm here. I'm Kalesh, and I went through it. The whole damn thing. The spiritual teacher who survived kundalini crisis? That's me. And I'm gonna tell you what I learned, raw and unfiltered, because you deserve that much.

You're not crazy. Not in the way you think you are, anyway. What you're experiencing, that terrifying, exhilarating, confusing mess, it has a name. It's called kundalini awakening, and sometimes, it goes sideways. Sometimes it becomes a crisis. You feel like your body is on fire, your mind is racing with thoughts that aren't yours, you're seeing things, hearing things, feeling things that defy explanation. You're probably exhausted but can't sleep. You're probably starving but can't eat. And you're alone. Right?

I know that feeling. That utter, soul-crushing isolation. Nobody understands. Your friends look at you like you're speaking a foreign language, your family thinks you need a doctor - the kind with prescriptions. And maybe you do, for a bit, to get some sleep, to calm the nervous system. But they don't get the spiritual component, the deep, existential shaking that's happening. They don't get that it feels like you're dying, but also being born. It's a hell of a ride, and you didn't sign up for the express train, did you? Nobody does.

The Shock of Awakening: When the "Good" Goes Bad

I'll tell you what, when I first started on my spiritual path, I was all about the bliss, the peace, the light. That's what they sell you, isn't it? The gurus, the books, the workshops. They talk about awakening like it's a walk in the park, a gentle unfolding. They don't tell you about the dark night of the soul that can last years. They don't tell you about the body tremors, the spontaneous movements, the intense heat, the visions that make you question your sanity. They don't tell you about the purging. Oh god, the purging.

My crisis started subtly, like a low hum in my spine. I thought it was cool. Like, "Oh, I'm making progress! This is it!" Then it escalated. Fast. It felt like an electrical current, surging through me, uncontrollably. My body would jerk and twitch. I'd have these intense energetic releases that felt like orgasms, but they weren't sexual. They were just pure energy. I'd be sitting, trying to meditate, and suddenly my body would twist into these bizarre yoga poses I'd never learned. It was terrifying because I had no control. None. My mind felt like a thousand radios playing at once, tuned to different stations. I couldn't focus. I couldn't hold a conversation. I couldn't sleep for days on end.

I thought I was having a psychotic break. Honestly, I did. I was convinced I'd broken something in my brain, that I'd pushed too hard, too fast. I went to doctors, therapists, anyone who would listen. They looked at me with concern, prescribed meds, but nothing touched the core of what was happening. They couldn't see the energy, couldn't feel the fire. They couldn't comprehend that this wasn't just mental illness - it was a spiritual process gone haywire. Know what I mean? It's like trying to explain the color red to someone born blind. You just can't.

The Solitude of the Seeker

And that's where the isolation really kicks in. You're out there, flailing, and the people who are supposed to help you don't have the map. They don't even know the territory exists. You start to question everything. Your beliefs, your sanity, your purpose. You feel utterly, completely alone in this vast, terrifying cosmic joke. You wonder if you're being punished. You wonder if you're special, or just a freak. It's a brutal place to be, and I wouldn't wish it on my worst enemy.

I remember one night, I was lying on the floor, shaking uncontrollably, feeling like my head was going to explode. I called a friend, someone I thought was "spiritual." I tried to explain, stumbling over words, trying to make sense of the indescribable. Her response? "Oh, you're just stressed. Maybe you should try some chamomile tea." Chamomile tea. I almost laughed, but I was too busy crying. That was when I knew I was truly on my own. You're probably feeling that now, aren't you? That sting of misunderstanding, that hollow ache of being unseen. It's a dark place, but you're not the first to walk it, and you won't be the last. I'm telling you this so you know you're not imagining it. It's real. And you can get through it.

The Hard-Won Lessons: What I Learned on the Other Side

So, what did I learn from all that hell? A lot. More than I could ever put into words, but I'll try to give you the cliff notes, the stuff I wish someone had told me at 3 AM when I was losing my mind.

You Are Not Your Experience

This is probably the biggest one. When you're in the thick of it, you become the sensations, the thoughts, the fear. You identify with it so deeply that you forget there's a "you" separate from all that. But there is. You are the awareness witnessing it all. The fire, the fear, the visions - they are happening to you, but they are not you. This distinction is tiny, but it's everything. It gives you a sliver of distance, a breathing room. It doesn't make the sensations go away, but it changes your relationship to them. You're not drowning; you're watching the waves. That's a huge shift. Look, I know it's hard to grasp when you feel like you're being torn apart, but try. Just try to remember that observer part of you. It's always there, quiet, watching.

I also learned that you absolutely need to read about this stuff. Not just the fluffy, feel-good spiritual books. You need to read the real accounts, the ones that acknowledge the dark side. Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) by Stanislav Grof and Christina Grof was a lifesaver for me. It made me feel seen, like someone finally understood. Also, The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) gave me a lot of context. These books don't offer a magic cure, but they offer validation, and when you're feeling insane, validation is gold. It's proof you're not alone, that others have walked this path and survived.

Grounding is Your Best Friend

When you're flying high on energetic surges, you need to pull yourself back down to earth. This isn't about stopping the process - you can't, not really - but about managing its intensity. My energy was constantly trying to shoot out of my head, and I felt like I was going to explode. I learned to focus on my feet, on the ground beneath me. Literally. Walking barefoot on grass, sitting with my back against a tree, eating heavy, root vegetables. Anything that made me feel connected to the physical world. Hot baths with Epsom salts, deep breathing into my belly, even just lying flat on the floor and feeling the earth holding me. These things aren't "spiritual" in the traditional sense, but they are absolutely vital when your spirit is going haywire. You've got to take care of the body, because that's where you're experiencing all of this. Your body is your anchor. Don't forget it.

Seek the Right Support

I mentioned how isolating it is, right? That's because most people don't get it. But there are people who do. You need to find them. Not just any therapist, but one who understands spiritual emergence. Not just any spiritual teacher, but one who has actually walked through the fire, not just read about it in a book. It's hard to find them, I'm not gonna lie. But they exist. Look for people who talk about the shadow, about the difficulties, about the messy parts of awakening. They're the ones who won't just dismiss you. They're the ones who can hold space for you without judgment. Bonnie Greenwell's books, like When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link), were also incredibly helpful. She's one of those people who gets it.

And sometimes, yes, you do need medical help. If you can't sleep for days, if you're having severe panic attacks, if you're truly feeling like you might harm yourself, go to a doctor. Get some temporary relief. It doesn't mean you're a failure, or that your spiritual experience isn't real. It means you're human, and your body needs support. Sometimes the spiritual process is so intense that the physical body can't keep up. There's no shame in getting help to stabilize yourself. You can always get off the meds later, once you're more stable. Your primary goal right now is survival. Period.

Acceptance and Surrender: The Only Way Through

This one's tough, especially when you're terrified. You want it to stop, right? You want to go back to "normal." But here's the truth: you can't. Not really. Once the process has started, it's like a river that's burst its banks. You can't put the water back. The only way through is to surrender. To accept that this is happening, and that you have to let it unfold. It's not about giving up, it's about letting go of control. It's about trusting, even when every fiber of your being screams in protest, that there's a deeper intelligence at play. That this chaos has a purpose, even if you can't see it now.

I spent so much time fighting it. Trying to make it stop, trying to control the sensations, trying to silence the thoughts. And all that did was make it worse. It was like trying to hold a beach ball underwater. The harder I pushed, the more force it came back with. It was only when I started to truly accept, to say "Okay, this is happening, and I can't stop it, so what now?" - that's when things started to shift, ever so slightly. That's when I could begin to ride the waves instead of being crushed by them. Does that land for you? It's a hard pill to swallow, I know. But it's the truth.

Surrender doesn't mean you don't take action. It means you take action from a place of acceptance, not resistance. It means you find those grounding practices, you seek out the right support, you read the books, you do what you can to manage the experience, but you let go of the idea that you can force it to stop or go away. You can't. It's a deep, deep cleansing, a rewiring, and it has to run its course. Your job is to be present for it, to witness it, and to take care of yourself as best you can while it unfolds.

The Aftermath: Life Beyond Crisis

So yeah, I survived. And you will too. It wasn't a quick fix, it wasn't a sudden "poof, I'm healed!" moment. It was a long, messy, agonizing process of integration. Of slowly, painstakingly, putting myself back together, but differently. I'm not the same person I was before. And honestly, I wouldn't want to be. The crisis stripped away everything that wasn't real, everything that wasn't truly me. It was brutal, but it was also the most deep spiritual experience of my life.

Now, I live with a deeper understanding of myself, of reality, of the incredible power that lies within us. I'm more compassionate, more patient, and way less judgmental. Because I know what it's like to walk through hell and come out the other side. I know what it's like to feel like you're losing your mind, and to discover that you were actually finding your soul. The fear is still there sometimes, the memory of the intensity. But it doesn't control me anymore. I've learned to live with it, to respect the power of this energy, and to integrate it into my daily life.

My nervous system is still sensitive, I'm more aware of energy than ever before. But I've learned to manage it. I have my grounding practices, my meditation, my quiet time. I've built a life that supports this new, more expanded version of me. And you can too. It takes time. It takes patience. It takes an incredible amount of courage. But you have that courage. You wouldn't be reading this at 3 AM if you didn't.

So, take a deep breath. You're not alone. What you're experiencing is real. And you can get through this. Find your support, ground yourself, surrender to the process, and remember that you are the awareness witnessing it all. You're stronger than you think. I know you are. I've been there.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
